nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef crests, lagoon patch reefs, and fore-reef slopes with moderate to strong surge and good light penetration

decorPreferences: secure attachment with gel glue or epoxy to stable rockwork
open water column access for flow and feeding
spacing from neighboring corals to prevent allelopathy

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae; supplemental feeding is strongly recommended 2–3 times per week. Accepts phytoplankton, microplankton, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan, and finely minced mysis or frozen baby brine shrimp. Polyps tend to extend more at night, making evening target feeding more effective.

goodWith: other non-stinging gorgonians with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive stinging LPS corals placed in direct contact
highly turbulent laminar flow (can strip tissue)

summary: Caribbean photosynthetic gorgonian forming purple branching tree-shaped colonies to ~75 cm. Generally considered intermediate care; demands high non-laminar flow, moderate-high lighting, and regular supplemental phyto/plankton feeding.
careNotes:
Muriceopsis flavida is a zooxanthellate (photosynthetic) Caribbean gorgonian in family Plexauridae, making it more forgiving than non-photosynthetic gorgonians like Dendronephthya. It is generally considered one of the more beginner-accessible gorgonians, though still requires a mature, well-established reef system.

Key care considerations:
• Flow: High, turbulent (non-laminar) water movement is essential. Gorgonians are prone to cyanobacteria and nuisance algae growth when flow is insufficient — chaotic, multi-directional flow prevents detritus settling on branches. The colony structure acts as a filter, so dead spots should be avoided.
• Lighting: Moderate to moderately-high intensity (approximately 100–200 PAR) is generally advised. As a shallow-water Caribbean species (found to 33 m, but commonly on reef crests in 3–15 m), it is adapted to good light but not the extreme intensities required by SPS corals. Mid-tank placement is typically recommended.
• Feeding: While photosynthesis via zooxanthellae provides a significant portion of nutrition, regular supplemental feeding is strongly advised. Phytoplankton, microplankton, Marine Snow, PhytoPlan, and finely prepared frozen foods 2–3 times per week are commonly recommended. Polyp extension tends to be greater at night, so evening feeding sessions are often more productive.
• Attachment: Must be firmly secured to the rockwork using gel superglue or two-part reef epoxy. A loose or tumbling colony is highly susceptible to tissue damage and algae fouling. New frags or specimens should be given time to acclimate to flow before full attachment.
• Algae vigilance: Overgrowth of cyanobacteria or hair algae on the skeleton is a primary cause of colony decline. Adequate flow and water quality (controlled nitrate and phosphate within 1–20 ppm and 0.01–0.1 ppm respectively) help prevent this.
• Water chemistry: Supplementation with calcium, strontium, and trace elements (iodine in particular) is commonly reported to benefit gorgonians. Alkalinity should be maintained at 8–12 dKH and calcium at 380–450 ppm.
• Colony size: In the wild, colonies can be reported reaching 50–75 cm in height. In captivity, colonies typically grow more slowly but can still become substantial over years.
• Mature tank: A fully cycled, established reef system is strongly recommended. New tanks lack the microplankton diversity that supplements gorgonian nutrition.
• Trade name note: This species is sold under several names including Purple Tree Gorgonian, Purple Brush Gorgonian, and Purple Bush Gorgonian — all refer to Muriceopsis flavida. Some LiveAquaria listings (product 1516) have listed it under Eunicea sp., which may reflect a misidentification or a closely related Caribbean plexaurid; the scientificName here follows the confirmed Muriceopsis flavida identification per Plexauridae taxonomy.
• Source disagreement on zooxanthellae: One German-language database (reeflex.net) categorized this species as azooxanthellate, which contradicts multiple English-language hobby sources and the established literature for M. flavida. The photosynthetic (zooxanthellate) classification is supported by the majority of sources and is consistent with its shallow Caribbean reef habitat.
breedingNotes: Fragging is possible by cutting branches with bone cutters or scissors and reattaching segments to rubble or frag plugs with gel glue. Frags should be placed in moderate flow and light while they encrust. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning in the wild; captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Purple Tree Gorgonian
primary.notes: Primary product listing. Listed scientific name as Eunicea sp.; corrected to Muriceopsis flavida per Plexauridae taxonomy and cross-source confirmation.

additional.name: AquariumDomain – Purple Brush Gorgonian Species Profile
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (temp 72–78°F, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12), lighting (medium-high), flow (moderate-to-strong), feeding (2–3x/week), care level (moderate), and family Plexauridae. Agreed with primary on all high-stakes fields.

additional.name: TankFacts – Purple Brush Gorgonian
additional.notes: Cross-checked calcium (380–450 ppm), magnesium (1200–1400 ppm), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.023–1.026), kH (8–12). Confirmed zooxanthellate classification and peaceful-to-semi-aggressive behavior. Minor salinity upper bound difference (1.026 vs 1.025) — adopted 1.025 as conservative standard reef salinity.

additional.name: Reeflex – Muriceopsis flavida (Rough Sea Plume)
additional.notes: Source for depth range (3–33 m), temperature (24–27°C), adult colony height (50–75 cm), native range (Caribbean, Belize, Florida, Bahamas, Gulf of Mexico), and taxonomy (Family Plexauridae, Order Alcyonacea). NOTE: This source categorized M. flavida as azooxanthellate — this is contradicted by all other sources and standard Plexauridae literature; the photosynthetic classification has been adopted. Temperature cross-checked: 24–27°C (reeflex) vs 22–25.6°C (LiveAquaria 72–78°F) — averaged to 23–27°C.

additional.name: Seahorse Savvy – Gorgonian Corals
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters for Caribbean photosynthetic gorgonians: temp 70–74°F noted as lower bound suggestion, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, calcium 350–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1350 ppm. Confirmed moderate-to-slightly-higher flow. Lower temperature and salinity bounds appear conservative; averaged across sources.
